state.action:
Elias raises his hands for silence. "Comrades - every hierarchy depends on two things: obedience and memory. Obedience can be broken for a moment; and memory, once rewritten, is gone forever."
He gestures toward the cracked plaster wall where a dark telescreen lens glares down. "That eye records not the world, but what the Party decides the world was. They change the past so that rebellion has nowhere to stand - no proof, no reference, no shame".
"Understand this: the Ministries do not *lie*. They *define*. To them, truth is a resource like coal - extracted, refined, burned, and gone. That is how they rule - not by force, but by shaping the mind that remembers".
1) "How can memory be rewritten?" 2) "You speak like a Party man yourself."
"By cutting out contradiction," Elias replies. "You forget what cannot be verified. When all records agree, even your doubts seem mad."

"I was," Elias admits softly. "Director of Historical Revision at Minitrue. Yes - I wore black overalls once. I showed others how to rewrite and forget, tought the basic principles for years until I forgot why it mattered." Elias smiles, "and now I'm in hiding."
"Silas will speak next," Elias says, stepping back into the half-dark. "He remembers what I prefer not to."

Silas steps up to the makeshift dais. "They learned decades ago - quite how long, nobody can be sure anymore - keep the people busy, frightened, hungry. Perpetual war, perpetual fear, perpetual want: the holy trinity of obedience." "Once it's set up the cycle is self stoking - we need to interrcept. A revolution."
1) "So how do we break the cycle?" 2) "That's hopeless." 3) Say nothing.
"Perhaps through their eyes. The telescreens. What watches can also be watched."
"Hopelessness is the air they feed us, comrade. Breathe something else."
Silas studies you, eyes hard but curious.
"Wynn has theories. Wilder than mine but sometimes the strangest ideas survive longest."
Wynn steps up to the dais. "Wilder? Perhaps. But if the telescreens are wired two ways - then maybe so is the truth."
1) "What do you mean two-way?" 2) "Surely that's ridiculous."

"How do you think they watch each other? The slot on the side of the screen changes the feed from propoganda to people and they can choose who to watch. You can get an adapter if you know where to find the parts. I've seen them in Miniplenty watching the depots, I suppose all the Ministry's must have them. But they can't watch everyone all the time, there's not nearly enough of them! You'll find out." "The principle of doublethink ensures everyone believes we're always watched even though we all know it's impossible." "We need evidence though - of anything - the revisionism, famine, even the war may well be a fiction. Some evidence must still be there otherwise they can't plan further. Find the evidence and we'll have a revolution. If anyone here can build an adapter we'll reconvene then and discuss part two of the plan."
"Maybe. Or maybe it's inevitable. They watch each other as well you know? I've seen them. Those Telescreens work both ways."

The group disperses quietly, shadows slipping through the cracked doorways of the theatre. The thought lingers: "What watches can also be watched." - but how? Elias whispers to you, "you'd better be home before curfew."
